title: Partial Fixes for Mode Collapse
concept: There's no single fix for mode collapse — techniques like minibatch discrimination and unrolled GANs offer partial mitigation, but it remains an open, actively-researched problem, one of several training struggles that motivate the next failure mode: vanishing gradients.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's the practical way to check whether your GAN has mode-collapsed?

options.text: Generate a large batch of samples and visually check for repetition
Check only the final loss number
Assume it's fine if training finishes without errors

exercises.explain: Right — a visual batch check, since loss numbers alone can be misleading. Today: what can actually be done about it once you spot it.

cards.title: No single silver bullet
cards.body: Once you can spot mode collapse, the obvious next question is: how do you stop it? The honest answer is that there's no one fix that reliably solves it — just a handful of partial mitigations researchers have developed, each helping somewhat, none guaranteeing a cure.

cards.title: Minibatch discrimination
cards.body: One popular idea: let the discriminator compare samples within the same batch to each other, not just judge each one in isolation. If it notices a batch is suspiciously similar to itself, it can flag that — giving the generator pressure to diversify.

cards.title: Extending the lineup trick
cards.body: Remember the detective's lineup from last lesson — repeats are obvious once you compare photos side by side? Minibatch discrimination gives the discriminator that same lineup power: instead of judging one fake at a time, it gets to compare several candidates side-by-side and notice when they're suspiciously identical.

cards.title: Unrolled GANs: peeking ahead
cards.body: Another approach, unrolled GANs, lets the generator preview several future discriminator updates before committing to a move — like a weather forecaster checking several days ahead instead of reacting only to today's forecast. This discourages the generator from exploiting a discriminator weakness that's about to disappear anyway.

cards.title: "So is mode collapse solved now?"
cards.body: Not even close. Minibatch discrimination, unrolled GANs, and other tricks (like feature matching or replaying older generated samples) are partial mitigations, not guarantees — and they often trade off against training speed or stability. Mode collapse remains an open, actively-researched problem in GAN training.

left.text:
Generator narrows to
one winning output
No full fix exists yet

exercises.prompt: What does minibatch discrimination let the discriminator do?

options.text: Compare multiple samples within the same batch to each other, to spot suspicious repetition
Train ten times faster than normal

exercises.explain: Minibatch discrimination gives the discriminator a wider view — comparing samples to each other, not just judging each one alone — so repetition becomes visible to it.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Techniques like minibatch discrimination and unrolled GANs completely and permanently solve mode collapse.
exercises.explain: False. They're partial mitigations that help reduce the RISK of mode collapse — mode collapse remains an open, actively-researched problem, not a solved one.

exercises.sentence: Unrolled GANs let the generator preview several future discriminator updates before committing, similar to a forecaster checking several days ___ instead of just today.
exercises.explain: Peeking ahead at where the discriminator is heading discourages the generator from exploiting a weakness that's about to vanish.

exercises.prompt: Which statement accurately reflects the current state of mode collapse research?

options.text: There are several partial mitigation techniques, but mode collapse remains an open, actively-researched problem
Mode collapse was fully solved by minibatch discrimination alone
Mode collapse only affected early GANs and no longer occurs in any modern architecture

exercises.explain: Accurate framing: partial tools exist, but no complete fix — this is still active research territory.

exercises.prompt: You've now seen mode collapse end-to-end: what it is, a concrete digit example, why it happens, how to spot it, and some partial mitigations. But even a generator producing perfectly diverse output can hit a completely different wall: what happens when the discriminator's feedback signal itself goes quiet? What's the natural next topic?

options.text: Vanishing gradients — when the generator stops getting a useful learning signal from the discriminator at all
How to compress trained models for mobile devices

exercises.explain: Exactly! Mode collapse is one way a GAN can quietly fail even while the loss numbers look fine. Next up, Unit 207: Vanishing Gradients in GAN Training — a totally different failure mode lurking in the same competition.
